ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for North Indian mutton tawa curry. FOODON_03302757 (lamb dish) is used as the closest broader class. A more specific term for spiced lamb curry preparations is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat dishes — lamb and mutton preparations
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM covers lamb and mutton meat dishes broadly. No specific code for North Indian tawa-style mutton curry exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue; this is the closest defensible parent node.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to North Indian mutton tawa curry as a food entity. SNOMED CT food coding is not granular enough for this dish; null is appropriate.

preparation_method: spiced with whole and ground masala

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Spicy Mutton Tawa Curry is approximately 300 g, comprising bone-in or boneless mutton pieces with masala gravy served sizzling on a tawa plate.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton curry (cooked) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Table 1: Flesh Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports cooked mutton curry (bone-in, with onion-tomato masala) per 100 g edible portion. Values adapted to represent the spicy tawa preparation style; the macronutrient profile is consistent with a dry-finish tawa curry using similar ingredients. Sodium is elevated slightly to reflect restaurant-level seasoning. No exact entry for 'Mama Mia Mutton Tawa Curry' exists in any approved database; ICMR-NIN cooked mutton curry is the closest defensible primary source for an Indian lamb/mutton curry dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat curry dishes — analogous estimate based on lamb curry GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Spicy Mutton Tawa Curry. The GI is estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous meat curry dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008). Meat-based curries with minimal starchy carbohydrate and high protein and fat content consistently yield low GI values (GI 25–35). Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (30 × 3.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 3.15, rounded to 3.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from restaurant-level seasoning and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium (over half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg), so individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt, limit portion size, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and low net carbohydrate content (~3.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Spicy Mutton Tawa Curry has a low GI (~30) and minimal carbohydrate load, making it a suitable protein-rich main dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled and paired with a low-GI carbohydrate such as roti or cauliflower rice rather than white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lamb and cooking fat, and elevated restaurant sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 300 g serving contributes approximately 11.4 g saturated fat and 1260 mg sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency to once weekly, choose leaner cuts, and request preparation with minimal added ghee or oil to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Mutton Tawa Curry is a North Indian restaurant dish consisting of succulent bone-in or boneless lamb (mutton) pieces cooked with finely chopped onions, tomatoes, ginger, garlic, green chillies, and a blend of whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, red chilli powder, turmeric, and garam masala. The dish is finished and served sizzling on a flat iron griddle called a tawa, which imparts a characteristic dry-roasted, slightly charred edge to the meat and concentrates the masala coating. The flavour profile is bold, spicy, and deeply savoury, with the heat from red and green chillies balanced by the umami richness of slow-cooked onion-tomato gravy. The texture of the meat is tender and juicy inside with a lightly caramelised exterior. In Singapore, this dish is served at North Indian restaurants such as Bawarchi, Bombay Curry and Kebabs, and Aryana, typically priced around SGD 19.90 per portion. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 165 kcal, 15.8 g protein, 10.2 g fat (3.8 g saturated), and only 3.5 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate option. The glycemic index is estimated at 30 (low), and the glycemic load per 300 g serving is approximately 3.2, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is elevated at approximately 420 mg per 100 g due to restaurant-level seasoning, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-certified in most North Indian Muslim-run restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mutton tawa curry?
Is tawa mutton curry high in sodium?
North Indian mutton curry calories Singapore

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 'Mama Mia Mutton Tawa Curry' or 'Spicy Mutton Tawa Curry' exists in any approved primary database. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked mutton curry (bone-in, onion-tomato masala), which is the closest defensible primary source for a North Indian lamb/mutton curry. GI is estimated from analogous meat curry data in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Confidence is medium rather than low because the ICMR-NIN source is a direct regional primary source for this cuisine category and the dish composition closely matches the described preparation.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN cooked mutton curry entry and is not reliably measurable from the described preparation. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for cooked mutton curry. 4. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with numeric estimates from ICMR-NIN and supporting USDA SR Legacy data for lamb shoulder cooked. 5. Sodium is elevated above the ICMR-NIN baseline to reflect restaurant-level seasoning typical of Singapore North Indian restaurant preparations. 6. The food_name 'Spicy Mutton Tawa Curry' is used as the canonical English name in place of the promotional menu name 'Mama Mia Mutton (Chef Recommendation) (Spicy)'; the original menu name is preserved in aliases. 7. Halal status is flagged as likely true given the restaurant context (North Indian Muslim-run establishments in Singapore) but is not formally verified per HPB or MUIS records; this flag should be confirmed before production use. 8. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous meat curry dishes; glycemic load per 300 g serving = (30 × 3.5 × 3) / 100 = 3.15, rounded to 3.2.
